name: Sweden (1844-1905)
description: A Swedish swallow-tailed flag — blue field with a yellow Nordic cross and a triangular tongue extending from the cross — defaced in the upper hoist canton with the Norwegian-Swedish union mark, a small chequered emblem combining the colours and saltires of Sweden and Norway.
history: Adopted on 20 June 1844 by King Oscar I as part of a unified flag system for the United Kingdoms of Sweden and Norway, replacing the plain blue-and-yellow flag of 1815. The new design added the union mark (a quartered field combining a Swedish blue saltire on yellow and a Norwegian red saltire on white, with white fimbriation) to the canton of both Swedish and Norwegian flags. The mark was deeply unpopular in both countries — particularly in Norway, where it was nicknamed the 'sillsalladen' ('herring salad'). Norway removed it from its merchant flag in 1899 and from all flags in 1898-1899; Sweden retained the mark on its flags until the dissolution of the union, removing it on 1 November 1905 after the union ended.

features.symbolism: the historic Swedish national colours, derived from the royal coat of arms
the union between Sweden and Norway, combining the colours and saltires of both kingdoms; popularly nicknamed the sillsallad ('herring salad') by its critics
the personal union of Sweden and Norway
